name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a Java library in the Machai ecosystem that provides a provider-agnostic integration layer for generative AI and embedding workflows. It enables Java applications to interact with large language models and related AI services without coupling application code to a specific vendor SDK, authentication flow, endpoint format, or execution model. The library centralizes provider resolution, model selection, prompt and instruction collection, optional host-side function tools, embedding requests, and token-usage reporting. Based on the published Javadocs, it includes a common provider contract in org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai, embedding support through org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider, provider resolution and usage aggregation through org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager, and reusable tool infrastructure in org.machanism.machai.ai.tools. Concrete integrations cover OpenAI-compatible services, Anthropic Claude, and EPAM CodeMie. The library supports request-scoped conversational workflows, prompt accumulation, instructions, optional file and URL inputs, structured local tool execution, embedding generation for semantic search and retrieval, and cross-request usage tracking. It is suitable for enterprise AI integration, agent-style workflows, semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, automated content generation, intelligent project assembly, and modular applications that need to switch AI backends through configuration rather than source code changes.

usageContext: Java applications that need a provider-neutral API for conversational AI and embeddings
Systems that must switch between OpenAI, Anthropic, and CodeMie backends through configuration
Applications exposing controlled local Java functions to LLMs through tool-calling workflows
Enterprise services that need prompt execution, embedding generation, and token-usage reporting
Semantic search, retrieval, ranking, and vector indexing scenarios
Machai modules and other JVM applications that need request-scoped generative AI integration

constructors.description: Creates and initializes a conversational AI provider from a provider-qualified model identifier such as OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini or Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet and a runtime Configurator. This is the primary provider-neutral entry point for applications that want to choose an AI backend at runtime without binding to a specific implementation class.

examples.description: How to use it: first add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.2.0, then configure the provider credential in Configurator, and finally resolve the provider through GenaiProviderManager. This is the standard installation and bootstrap path for chat-style AI requests.

constructors.description: Creates and initializes an embedding-capable provider from a provider-qualified embedding model identifier. Use this constructor-style factory when the application needs vector generation for semantic retrieval, similarity search, or indexing workflows.

examples.description: How to use it: install the library as a Maven dependency, configure the credential for the embedding backend, and resolve an EmbeddingProvider through the manager. This is the recommended setup for semantic search and retrieval pipelines.

constructors.description: Constructs a loader for service-discovered function tools and prompts. Applications use this component to install compatible host-side capabilities onto a provider after provider creation and before request execution.

examples.description: How to use it: after adding the dependency and preparing your provider, create FunctionToolsLoader, then apply discovered tools to the provider so the model can use application-defined capabilities during execution.

features.name: prompt
features.description: Adds a user prompt to the current request-scoped provider session. Prompts can be accumulated before execution, allowing applications to build a conversational request incrementally.

examples.description: Practical how-to scenario: install the library in Maven, configure OPENAI_API_KEY, resolve a provider, set an instruction, add a prompt, and execute the request. This demonstrates the minimal conversational workflow.

features.name: instructions
features.description: Sets system-level or session-level instructions that guide model behavior for the current request. Use it to define tone, response constraints, role, or execution policy before prompting.

examples.description: Practical how-to scenario: install the dependency, configure the Anthropic credential, create the provider, define stable instructions, and then run prompts that should follow those constraints.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes the currently prepared request using the selected provider, accumulated prompts, registered tools, and runtime configuration. Depending on the backend, execution may include tool-calling loops and usage collection before returning the final response text.

examples.description: Practical how-to scenario: after installation and configuration, prepare the request and call perform(). This is the step that sends the request to the selected AI backend and returns the generated result.

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Discovers compatible tool contributors through Java ServiceLoader infrastructure and applies them to the provider. This allows applications to expose reusable, host-side capabilities to the model only when needed.

examples.description: Step-by-step how to use it: 1) install the library, 2) package FunctionTools implementations with ServiceLoader registration, 3) create the provider, 4) create FunctionToolsLoader, 5) apply tools, and 6) run a prompt that can call those tools.

features.name: embedding
features.signature: public abstract java.util.List<java.lang.Double> org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider.embedding(java.lang.String text, long dimensions)
features.description: Generates an embedding vector for the supplied text using the configured embedding backend. The result can be used for semantic search, similarity comparison, retrieval, clustering, and ranking workflows.

examples.description: Practical how-to scenario: add the Maven dependency, configure the embedding provider credential, resolve an EmbeddingProvider, and generate vectors for documents or queries that will be stored in a semantic index.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
customizations.description: Primary extension point for implementing new conversational AI backends behind the common provider abstraction. Custom implementations can define initialization, prompt handling, tool registration, execution, and request lifecycle behavior while remaining compatible with application code that depends only on the Genai API.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement the Genai contract for a new backend, package it in your application or integration module, and keep the rest of your application dependent on the interface instead of a vendor SDK.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
customizations.description: Extension contract for integrating new embedding-capable providers into the same provider-neutral ecosystem. Implement this interface if you want to add vector generation support backed by a different AI service.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement EmbeddingProvider for a new vector backend, initialize it with model and configuration, and use it in retrieval or semantic search scenarios.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: Service-provider extension point for reusable host-side tool bundles and prompt contributors. Implementations can be discovered through ServiceLoader and applied to compatible providers at runtime.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement the SPI, register it under META-INF/services, add it to the application classpath, and let FunctionToolsLoader discover and apply it during provider setup.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Core provider-neutral contract for conversational AI workflows, including initialization, prompt accumulation, instruction handling, tool registration, execution, and lifecycle operations.

examples.description: How to use it: keep application code dependent on the Genai interface so provider selection and backend changes remain configuration-driven.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Provider-neutral abstraction for text-to-vector generation used in semantic indexing, retrieval, ranking, and similarity search workflows.

examples.description: How to use it: resolve an embedding provider when your application needs semantic vectors without necessarily using conversational generation features.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: SPI contract for service-discovered host-side tool and prompt contributors that can be applied to compatible providers.

examples.description: How to use it: implement the interface, register it with ServiceLoader metadata, and package it on the classpath so the application can expose custom tools to the AI provider.

examples.title: Install the library with Maven and run a basic OpenAI prompt
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.2.0 to your pom.xml.
Run your application on a Java 8+ runtime.
Set the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable or provide the same value through org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator.
Create a Configurator and resolve a provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini", conf).
Call instructions() and prompt() to prepare the request.
Call perform() and process the returned answer.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
provider.instructions("You are a helpful Java assistant.");
provider.prompt("Explain what a provider abstraction solves in one paragraph.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A practical how-to scenario showing installation, credential configuration, provider creation, prompt execution, and result handling for a first conversational request.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run the library with Claude
steps: Add the genai-client Maven dependency to your project.
Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the environment or in Configurator.
Resolve the provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet", conf).
Set instructions appropriate for your task.
Submit one or more prompts and call perform() to execute the request.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet", conf);
provider.instructions("Return implementation guidance for a JVM service team.");
provider.prompt("Outline a migration plan from vendor-specific AI calls to a provider-neutral abstraction.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A real-world how-to scenario demonstrating installation, Anthropic configuration, provider resolution, and prompt execution through the same provider-neutral API.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run reusable tools through ServiceLoader
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build so provider and tool SPI APIs are available.
Create a class that implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools.
Register that implementation in META-INF/services so Java ServiceLoader can discover it.
Create and configure a provider through GenaiProviderManager.
Create org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader and call applyTools(provider).
Prompt the model with a task that benefits from installed tools, then call perform().
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

public final class com.example.tools.TimeFunctionTools implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools {
  public void applyTools(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider) {
    // Register application-specific tools here.
  }
}

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader tools = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
tools.applyTools(provider);
provider.instructions("Use installed tools when helpful.");
provider.prompt("Inspect the current environment and summarize it using available tools.");
java.lang.String result = provider.perform();
examples.description: A step-by-step how-to covering dependency installation, ServiceLoader registration, provider setup, tool application, and execution of a realistic tool-enabled workflow.

examples.title: Generate embeddings for semantic search and retrieval
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build.
Configure the credential for the embedding backend you want to use, such as OPENAI_API_KEY.
Resolve an embedding provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider("OpenAI:text-embedding-3-large", conf).
Call embedding(text, dimensions) for each document or query you want to vectorize.
Store the vectors in a search index, vector database, or similarity engine.
Generate a query embedding later and compare it against stored vectors to find relevant content.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider("OpenAI:text-embedding-3-large", conf);
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> documentVector = provider.embedding("Installation guide for the GenAI Client library", 3072L);
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> queryVector = provider.embedding("How do I configure tool support?", 3072L);
examples.description: A practical how-to scenario for semantic retrieval use cases, showing installation, provider configuration, vector generation, and the standard document-plus-query embedding workflow.

examples.title: Install and run the library with EPAM CodeMie
steps: Add the genai-client Maven dependency to your application.
Provide GENAI_USERNAME and GENAI_PASSWORD through environment variables or Configurator.
Optionally configure AUTH_URL if your environment uses a non-default CodeMie authentication endpoint.
Resolve the provider with a CodeMie-prefixed model such as CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini.
Call prompt() and perform() through the same provider-neutral API used for other backends.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("GENAI_USERNAME", java.lang.System.getenv("GENAI_USERNAME"));
conf.set("GENAI_PASSWORD", java.lang.System.getenv("GENAI_PASSWORD"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
provider.prompt("Create a short test plan for an HTTP client.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A practical how-to scenario for enterprise environments using EPAM CodeMie, showing installation, authentication setup, provider resolution, and execution through a common API.
